text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Donald Trump'], topics=['birthright citizenship', '14th amendment', 'immigration', 'executive order'].

## BACKGROUND (8)
- [2025-01-08] President-elect Donald Trump is preparing more than 100 executive orders, which he plans to launch starting on Day One of his new White House term, Inauguration Day, January 20, 2025. Trump informed Republican senators about the executive orders during a private meeting on Capitol Hill on January 8, 2025. Top adviser Stephen Miller outlined proposed border security and immigration enforcement measures. The planned executive actions are expected to cover a wide range of topics, including U.S.-Mexico border policies, energy development, federal workforce rules, school gender policies, and vaccine mandates. The new administration plans to rescind executive orders from the Biden administration and implement its own proposals, including completing the U.S.-Mexico border wall and establishing immigration detention facilities. An estimated $100 billion in funding is being sought for these initiatives. The administration also plans to revert to first-term border measures and deport an estimated 1 million migrants.
- [2025-01-08] U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's transition team and Republican allies cited recent Canadian border control changes as evidence of Trump's influence.

On Wednesday, January 8, 2025, Trump's transition team issued a press release titled "President Trump Is Securing The Border And He Hasn't Even Taken Office Yet."

Canada announced new measures on Tuesday, January 7, 2025, including new helicopters at the border, migration controls, money-laundering rules, and a joint Canada-U.S. anti-fentanyl police task force.

Incoming White House press secretary and Capitol Hill Republicans posted messages regarding these developments.

Trump posted on Truth Social on Wednesday, January 8, 2025, that Canada becoming the "51st state" is a "great idea" and repeated claims about U.S. subsidies to Canada.

It remains unclear if these developments will affect Trump's previously stated threat of a 25 percent tariff on Canada.
